name: Action Condensed
classification_notes: A condensed sans designed for interface and screen work, with four weights and three grades per weight so text can change weight on rollover without disrupting layout. Commercial Type describe it as casual and quirky for a straight-sided condensed sans.
summary: A condensed screen sans by Erik van Blokland, with grades for rollover swaps and a friendly, slightly quirky personality.
description: A condensed sans by Erik van Blokland, drawn for interface design without an overtly neutral personality. Four weights each have three grades on the same width, so text can change weight on rollover without disrupting the layout. Although built with screens and rollovers in mind, it holds up on posters as well as in browsers and phone interfaces, with a casual personality and quirky shapes that give it more warmth than most straight-sided condensed sans.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Action Condensed as a screen typeface by Dutch designer Erik van Blokland, made because he wanted interface typefaces without an overtly neutral personality. Each of the four weights has three grades on the same width for rollover effects, and the family is positioned to work as well on a poster as in a web browser or on a phone.

name: Action Text
classification_notes: A sans serif engineered for legibility at small sizes on screen and in challenging environments. Available as a variable font with weight and grade axes, plus a Flat version with Bright and Dark grades for smooth rollovers without changing copyfit.
summary: A screen sans by Erik van Blokland for small sizes and interfaces, with variable weight and grade axes and a Flat version for clean rollovers.
description: A sans serif by Erik van Blokland that puts his ideas about type for interfaces and continuous text onscreen into practice, building on Action Condensed. Letterforms come from function: details that could not be stripped away were simplified and exaggerated. The variable version carries weight and grade axes, and a Flat version with Bright and Dark grades holds the same horizontal space, which keeps rollovers smooth on screen and protects copyfit when weight changes in print. Aimed at the lineage of sans serifs engineered for legibility at tiny sizes and in difficult environments.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Action Text as a continuation of Erik van Blokland's exploration of type for interfaces and continuous onscreen text. They place it in the lineage of sans serifs engineered for legibility at tiny point sizes and in challenging environments, with a variable version carrying weight and grade axes and a Flat version designed for smooth onscreen rollovers and consistent copyfit in print.

name: Algebra
classification_notes: A broad-shouldered slab serif built on superelliptical forms, with two optical sizes. The designers describe it as a grotesk with serifs, referencing Egyptienne, Schadow and Melior. Flat vertices on diagonals give it a brutal character while keeping letters open at text sizes.
summary: A broad-shouldered slab on superelliptical forms by Bernau and Carvalho, drawn for editorial use with a crisp display cut and a calmer text companion.
description: A slab serif by Kai Bernau and Susana Carvalho that grew from Granger, a headline typeface they drew for the US edition of Esquire. Built on superelliptical forms in two optical sizes, Algebra Display is crisp and lively with brutalist details, while the text companion has looser spacing for a comfortable reading rhythm. The flat vertices where diagonals meet, as in the A and M, give it a brutal feeling against the bulky round shapes and stop letters clogging at small sizes on screen or under poor print conditions. References to Frutiger's Egyptienne, Trump's Schadow and Zapf's Melior place it in a contemporary magazine context.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Algebra as a broad-shouldered slab serif by Kai Bernau and Susana Carvalho, built on superelliptical forms in two optical sizes. They quote the designers describing it as a grotesk with serifs, with references to Frutiger's Egyptienne, Trump's Schadow and Zapf's Melior, and note that flat vertices give a brutal feeling while keeping letters open in tough printing conditions.

name: Align
classification_notes: A variable serif family that explores flatness, with shaved overshoots, soft rounded corners and blunt bracketed serifs that dwindle as weight increases. The heaviest weight loses serifs entirely.
summary: A variable text serif by Greg Gazdowicz built around flatness, with shaved overshoots and serifs that fade out as weight grows.
description: A variable serif by Greg Gazdowicz that takes urban signage as a cue, particularly the way applied vinyl stretches and compresses while staying readable. Round glyphs have their overshoots shaved off, producing a horizontal flatness offset by soft, rounded corners and blunt bracketed serifs. As weight increases the serifs progressively dwindle and disappear entirely at the heaviest weight. Sits between text and display, dense and dark but built to read at length.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Align as a variable serif family by Greg Gazdowicz that explores flatness as a concept and a way of being in the world. They cite everyday urban signage and stretched vinyl as inspirations, with shaved overshoots, rounded corners and bracketed serifs that fade as the family grows heavier, calling it dense, dark and eminently readable beneath its overt oddness.

name: Antique No 6
classification_notes: An idiosyncratic slab serif revival based on a face first seen in the 1870s from the Figgins foundry, later cast by Stevens, Shanks & Sons. Released under Commercial Classics, expanded from a single bold to seven weights and an original italic in 2020.
summary: A seven-weight revival by Paul Barnes of an 1870s Figgins slab, drawn for text but with enough character to lead headlines.
description: A slab serif revival by Paul Barnes for Commercial Classics, based on an idiosyncratic face first seen in the 1870s. Originally released by the Figgins foundry, it survived multiple changes of ownership to become one of the last faces cast by Stevens, Shanks & Sons at the end of the twentieth century. First brought back as an alternate Bold for Caslon Ionic, it expanded in 2020 to seven weights with an original italic. Designed for text but distinctive enough to carry headlines, especially where editorial work wants a slightly off-kilter Victorian voice.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type place Antique No. 6 within Commercial Classics, describing it as a revival of an idiosyncratic Figgins slab serif from the 1870s. They note it was first reintroduced as an alternate Bold for Caslon Ionic and later expanded to seven weights with an original italic in 2020, suitable for text but with character enough for headlines.

name: Atlas
classification_notes: Originally a corporate typeface for Munich Re, drawn to avoid contemporary corporate trends in favour of clear, optimistic Dutch Modernism. Includes a Grotesk and a monospaced Typewriter companion. Additional development with Irene Vlachou, Ilya Ruderman and Christian Schwartz.
summary: A Dutch Modernist grotesque drawn for Munich Re with a matching monospaced Typewriter companion, suited to reports and identity work.
description: A grotesque sans by Kai Bernau and Susana Carvalho, originally designed as a corporate typeface for Munich Re, one of the world's largest reinsurance firms. The brief deliberately avoided contemporary corporate trends in favour of the clear, optimistic tone of Dutch Modernism. The client produced hundreds of publications, reports and documents a year, and needed a face that held clarity in complex typographic situations across a well-considered range of weights, while saving space on the page. The retail family pairs Atlas Grotesk with Atlas Typewriter, a monospaced companion, and was developed further for Greek and Cyrillic with Irene Vlachou and Ilya Ruderman, plus collaboration with Christian Schwartz.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Atlas as a typeface first drawn as a corporate face for Munich Re, deliberately avoiding contemporary corporate trends in favour of the clear, optimistic tone of Dutch Modernism. They cite the client's need for clarity across hundreds of reports a year and economic setting, and credit Kai Bernau and Susana Carvalho as designers, with Irene Vlachou, Ilya Ruderman and Christian Schwartz contributing to the wider system.

name: Austin
classification_notes: A loose revival by Paul Barnes of the late 18th century typefaces of Richard Austin for the publisher John Bell. A British modern with Scotch Roman heritage, narrow without being overtly condensed. Developed with Hrvoje Zivcic and Berton Hasebe across multiple optical sizes from Hairline to Text.
summary: Paul Barnes's loose revival of Richard Austin's modern, drawn for Harper's & Queen and now a default for fashion and editorial work.
description: A modern serif by Paul Barnes drawn for the British style magazine Harper's & Queen. A loose revival of the late 18th century typefaces of Richard Austin for the publisher John Bell, who cut the first British modern and later the iconoclastic Scotch Roman. Narrow without being overtly condensed, with the styling and sheen of New York in the 1970s, it has become a default for fashion mastheads and high-contrast editorial work. Developed with Hrvoje Zivcic and Berton Hasebe.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Austin as a loose revival by Paul Barnes of the typefaces of Richard Austin from the late 18th century for John Bell, designed for the British style magazine Harper's & Queen. They call it narrow without being overtly condensed, a modern with the styling and sheen of New York in the 1970s, developed with Hrvoje Zivcic and Berton Hasebe.

name: Austin News
classification_notes: Adapted from Austin for Jon Hill's redesign of the Daily Telegraph in 2015. A collection of four closely related typefaces for headlines and text in print and screen, with compact ascenders and descenders for classic news proportions, plus a condensed cut.
summary: Paul Barnes's news cut of Austin, drawn for the 2015 Daily Telegraph redesign, with compact news proportions across headline, text and a condensed.
description: A news serif by Paul Barnes designed for Jon Hill's 2015 redesign of the Daily Telegraph. Adapted from the Austin family, originally drawn for magazines, Austin News has compact ascenders and descenders for tight leading: classic news proportions. Four closely related cuts cover headlines and text in both print and screen use, including a condensed for tighter measures. Loosely based on the 18th century typefaces cut by Richard Austin for John Bell, it holds an elegant Anglo charm with the clean finish appropriate to contemporary news media.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Austin News as a collection of four closely related typefaces for headlines and text in both print and screen, designed by Paul Barnes for Jon Hill's redesign of the Daily Telegraph in 2015. Adapted from Austin, it has compact ascenders and descenders for tight leading, classic news proportions, and an elegant Anglo charm with a clean contemporary finish.

name: Ayer
classification_notes: A condensed display family by Miguel Reyes, designed 2016 to 2019 for the large format fashion magazine W. Spans low contrast Deck to extreme high contrast Poster, with additional Poster italics including Cursive with swash capitals, Angular and a non-traditional Blackletter.
summary: Miguel Reyes's condensed fashion display drawn for W magazine, running from low contrast Deck to extreme contrast Poster with Cursive, Angular and Blackletter italics.
description: A condensed display family by Miguel Reyes, designed between 2016 and 2019 for W, the large format fashion magazine, at the request of creative director Cian Browne and art director Jeffrey Burch. Ayer Deck is a low contrast sans with gentle flaring; Ayer sits at higher contrast for use from around 60pt or for knocking heads out of dark backgrounds; Ayer Poster carries the extreme high contrast typical of fashion typefaces, with three further italic options: a chaotically beautiful Cursive with full swash capitals, the sharply stylish Angular, and Miguel's decidedly non-traditional take on Blackletter.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Ayer as an elegant condensed display typeface by Miguel Reyes, designed between 2016 and 2019 for W magazine. They list a low contrast Deck, a higher contrast Ayer, and the extreme high contrast Ayer Poster, with three additional italic styles: a Cursive with swash capitals, the sharply stylish Angular, and Miguel's non-traditional Blackletter.

name: Besley Shaded
classification_notes: A Commercial Classics revival of Two Lines Small Pica Grotesque, a condensed shaded sans first shown by Thorowgood and continued by his successor Robert Besley. The shadow is to the lower right, shallow, and without the bevelled angle of Caslon Antique Shaded, giving a gentler lift from the page.
summary: A revival of Thorowgood and Besley's shaded condensed grotesque from the 1830s, with a shallow lower-right shadow that lifts letters gently off the page.
description: A shaded display sans revived by Paul Barnes for Commercial Classics, based on Two Lines Small Pica Grotesque, a condensed shaded grotesque first shown by Thorowgood in the 1830s and continued by his successor Robert Besley. The shadow falls to the lower right, with a depth around three and a half times that of the outline, much shallower than Caslon Antique Shaded. There is no bevelled angle, so the letters lift gently from the page rather than reading as fully three-dimensional. The condensed forms have plenty of character: a G without a crossbar, an R tail that tapers to a flat point, and numerals that taper to flat ends where one would expect a closed curve.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type place Besley Shaded within Commercial Classics, framing it as the shaded variant of Two Lines Small Pica Grotesque, first shown by Thorowgood in 1834 and continued by Robert Besley. They detail the shallow lower-right shadow, the condensed character-full letterforms, and unusual numerals, noting it as an early step in shaded sans serifs before they fully matured.

name: Blake and Stephenson Sans Shaded
classification_notes: A Commercial Classics revival of a nineteenth century shaded sans, with a shallow outline that increases slightly to the bottom right. Cut at smaller sizes, originally used to stand in for hand-engravers' lettering on print and name cards.
summary: A revival of a nineteenth century shaded sans by Blake and Stephenson, with a shallow outline that lifts gently to the bottom right.
description: A shaded display sans revived by Paul Barnes for Commercial Classics, based on a nineteenth century type by the Sheffield foundry Blake and Stephenson. Type foundries had long imitated fashionable hand lettering, and during the nineteenth century one of the most popular forms was the engraver's lettering found at the bottom of prints and on name cards. Foundries copied these styles, and Blake and Stephenson Shaded and Caslon Doric Outline are two examples. Simple sans forms carry a shallow outline that increases slightly to the bottom right, lifting the letter gently off the page with less definition than other shaded faces. Cut at smaller sizes, it was designed to replace the work of hand engravers.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type place Blake and Stephenson Sans Shaded within Commercial Classics, describing it as part of the nineteenth century imitation of engraver's lettering on prints and name cards. They note the shallow outline increasing slightly to the bottom right and the smaller sizes at which it was cut, as a foundry-cast substitute for the hand-engraver's work.

name: Blanchard
classification_notes: A light weight clarendon designed for text, revived by Paul Barnes with Tim Ripper for Commercial Classics. Drawn from mid nineteenth century models that clearly informed the later typewriter faces of the same century.
summary: A light clarendon for text from Commercial Classics, drawn by Paul Barnes with Tim Ripper from mid nineteenth century models that pre-figured the typewriter faces.
description: A light weight clarendon for text by Paul Barnes with Tim Ripper, released through Commercial Classics. After the typefaces of the early nineteenth century kept getting bolder, it was inevitable that some would head the other way. Blanchard is one of these: a light clarendon drawn for text from mid nineteenth century models that clearly informed the typewriter faces released later in the same century. Three weights with italics keep it functional for editorial body, captions and quieter heading use.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type place Blanchard within Commercial Classics, describing it as a light weight clarendon designed for text from the middle of the nineteenth century, a counter to the relentless boldness of earlier century typefaces, and a clear model for the typewriter faces that came later. Designed by Paul Barnes with Tim Ripper.

name: Brunel
classification_notes: An Anglo variant of the modern style defined by Bodoni and Didot, based on types first cut for Elizabeth Caslon at the end of the eighteenth century. Expanded across multiple optical sizes from Hairline and Poster to Text, covering Roman through Black plus a hairline cut for the largest sizes. A founding release in Commercial Classics.
summary: Paul Barnes's Anglo modern at the heart of Commercial Classics, drawn from Elizabeth Caslon's late 18th century types and extended from a roman to a black with hairline cuts for the largest sizes.
description: A modern serif by Paul Barnes that sets itself up as the Anglo response to the modern style defined by the continental masters Bodoni and Didot. Based on designs first cut for Elizabeth Caslon at the end of the eighteenth century, Commercial Classics expanded it to a range of weights and optical sizes: from a roman to an emphatic black, and from text to a hairline for the largest sizes. Elegant yet hardworking, it sits at the heart of the first Commercial Classics releases and is suited to fashion, editorial and identity work that wants a British accent on the modern.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Brunel as the Anglo variant of the modern style defined by the continental masters Bodoni and Didot, based on designs first cut for Elizabeth Caslon at the end of the eighteenth century. They note that it was expanded to encompass a wide range of weights and sizes, from roman to emphatic black, and from a text design to a hairline for the largest sizes, calling it the heart of the first Commercial Classics releases.

name: Canela
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Canela as a graceful display typeface that defies traditional classifications, occupying an ambiguous space between sans and serif through vestigial flaring at stroke ends. Began as an interpretation of Caslon before Reyes shed the serifs to arrive at a monumental, stone-carved quality.
summary: A graceful flared display family from Miguel Reyes that sits between sans and serif, much loved in fashion and editorial work.
description: A graceful display family by Miguel Reyes that began as an interpretation of Caslon before he stripped back the serifs to leave only vestigial flaring, arriving at a monumental quality drawn from stone carving. Canela debuted in the fifth issue of Document Journal and quickly settled into fashion and editorial typography, delicate at its lightest weights and warmer and more confident as it swells to black. The collection spans Canela Display, Canela Deck, Canela Text and Canela Condensed across six weights with matching italics, plus Cyrillic and Greek extensions and a separate Canela Arabic.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Canela as a graceful display typeface that defies traditional classifications, drawn by Miguel Reyes with forms occupying an ambiguous space between sans and serif, soft and sharp. Began as an interpretation of Caslon before Reyes shed the serifs and arrived at a monumental quality influenced by his experience with stone carving, debuting in the fifth issue of Document Journal.

name: Canela Arabic
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Canela Arabic as an extension of Miguel Reyes's Canela drawing inspiration from both the Thuluth and Naskh traditions, reflecting the sobriety and refinement of its Latin counterpart while keeping a distinct expressive presence.
summary: An Arabic companion to Canela by Khajag Apelian and Wael Morcos, drawing on the Thuluth and Naskh traditions.
description: An Arabic family drawn by Khajag Apelian and Wael Morcos that extends Miguel Reyes's Canela by reaching back to the Thuluth and Naskh calligraphic traditions. While the design echoes the sober elegance of its Latin counterpart, it carries its own distinct expressive presence, supplied with a paired Latin so editorial and identity systems can move between scripts without losing voice.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Canela Arabic, drawn by Khajag Apelian and Wael Morcos, as an expansion of the crisp elegance of Miguel Reyes's Canela that draws inspiration from both the Thuluth and Naskh traditions, reflecting the sobriety and refinement of its Latin counterpart while having a distinct and expressive presence of its own.

name: Caponi
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Caponi as a Bodoni revival that expands the canon by reaching back to Bodoni's early career, when he was influenced by the Rococo of Pierre Simon Fournier, rather than the high-contrast late types most revivals focus on.
summary: A three-family Bodoni revival commissioned for Entertainment Weekly's twentieth anniversary, spanning Display, Slab and Text.
description: A modern serif system named for the late Amid Capeci, who commissioned it for his twentieth-anniversary revamp of Entertainment Weekly. Caponi expands the typical Bodoni revival by pulling on his early career, when he was shaped by the Rococo of Pierre Simon Fournier, rather than only his late high-contrast cuts. The three companion families divide the workload: Caponi Display is a traditional Modern for headlines, Caponi Slab is a low-contrast workhorse that punches at black weights for posters and pull quotes, and Caponi Text is a warmer Bodoni interpretation with quirky italics and subtly varying serifs for long reading.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Caponi as named for the late Amid Capeci, who commissioned it for his twentieth-anniversary revamp of Entertainment Weekly. They note that most contemporary Bodoni revivals focus on the elegant, high-contrast types he cut in the early nineteenth century, while Caponi takes its primary reference from his earlier career, when he had been greatly influenced by the Rococo style of the French printer Pierre Simon Fournier.

name: Caslon Doric
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Caslon Doric as a synthesis of nineteenth-century British sans serif innovations, building on the pioneering faces of Caslon and Figgins and the later embrace of lowercase forms for use at multiple sizes.
summary: A nineteenth-century British grotesque system spanning narrow to wide and text to display, drawn by Paul Barnes as part of the wider Caslon programme.
description: A nineteenth-century sans drawn by Paul Barnes that traces the British grotesque tradition from the pioneering faces of Caslon and Figgins in the 1810s and 1820s through to the lowercase, multi-size families that emerged later in the century. Caslon Doric synthesises that line of work into a system spanning narrow to wide and text to display, with Wide, Extended, Condensed, Condensed Round and Condensed Text widths plus Outline so editorial and identity systems can flex across mastheads, body text, signage and packaging.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Caslon Doric as embodying the natural progression of nineteenth-century innovations in type. They cite the pioneering faces of Caslon and Figgins in the second and third decades of the nineteenth century, the spread of sans serifs across Europe and the United States, and the later embrace of lowercase forms in British foundries, framing Caslon Doric as a synthesis of these styles from narrow to wide for use at all sizes.

name: Caslon Doric Outline
classification_notes: Commercial Type position Caslon Doric Outline as an outlined cut within the Caslon Doric system, intended for display use where a hollowed, nineteenth-century inflected grotesque is wanted.
summary: The outline cut within the Caslon Doric system, drawn for display work where a hollowed nineteenth-century sans is wanted.
description: An outline display sans by Paul Barnes that sits within the wider Caslon Doric system, hollowing out the grotesque skeleton for posters, mastheads and packaging where you want a nineteenth-century British sans treated as ornament rather than text. Reads as a companion to the standard Caslon Doric widths, useful for short bursts of display typography in editorial, identity and signage work.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type publish Caslon Doric Outline as part of the Caslon Doric system, alongside the standard, Wide, Extended, Condensed, Condensed Round and Condensed Text widths drawn by Paul Barnes.

name: Caslon French Antique
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Caslon French Antique as a less bizarre form of the reverse-contrast display face known as Italian, also referred to as French on the Continent, with thick horizontals at the tops and bottoms of bowls and heavy slab serifs.
summary: A tamer reverse-contrast display face within the Caslon programme, with thick horizontals and heavy slab serifs that work across sizes.
description: A reverse-contrast display face by Paul Barnes that takes the historic Italian, known as French on the Continent, and reins in the more bizarre extremes of the genre. The thick horizontals at the tops and bottoms of bowls and the heavy slab serifs give it more conventional weight distribution, which keeps it useful at a wide range of sizes for posters, packaging, wayfinding and editorial display work rooted in the nineteenth century.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Caslon French Antique as another, less bizarre form of the reverse-contrast display face known as Italian, also referred to as French on the Continent. They note that the more conventional approach to weight, giving thick horizontals at the tops and bottoms of bowls and heavy slab serifs, makes this variant on the genre useful at a wide range of sizes.

name: Caslon Ionic
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Caslon Ionic as bolder and more robust than the modern, yet lighter and more refined than the Egyptian, with a bracketed serif. They position it as a lesser-known nineteenth-century innovation alongside Thorowgood's Clarendon that greatly influenced the newspaper fonts of the next century.
summary: A revival of Caslon's Ionic No. 2, the bracketed-serif workhorse that shaped newspaper typography in the twentieth century.
description: A bracketed-serif workhorse by Paul Barnes that revives Caslon's Ionic No. 2, a lesser-known nineteenth-century innovation that sat between the modern and the Egyptian and went on to shape newspaper typography in the twentieth century. The family adds extra weights to the original and is published alongside a matching Egyptian companion, Antique No. 6, for editorial and identity work where you want a robust, legible serif with British nineteenth-century roots.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Caslon Ionic as bolder and more robust than the modern, yet lighter and more refined than the Egyptian. They cite the Ionic with its bracketed serif as another innovation of the nineteenth century, less known than Thorowgood's Clarendon, and call Caslon's Ionic No. 2 a superb example of the form that greatly influenced the newspaper fonts of the next century.

name: Caslon Italian
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Caslon Italian as a reverse-contrast display style introduced by Caslon in 1821, with the fatface's stress reversed so thins become thicks and thicks become thins. The original punches remain in St Bride library.
summary: A revival of Caslon's 1821 reverse-contrast Italian, one of the strangest episodes of nineteenth-century typefounding.
description: A reverse-contrast display face by Paul Barnes that revives Caslon's 1821 Italian, one of the stranger experiments of early nineteenth-century typefounding. By reversing the fatface's stress so thins become thicks and thicks become thins, the original produced an unusual effect that other foundries largely declined to copy. Sits at the heart of the Caslon revival programme alongside Caslon French Antique and Caslon Italian Shaded for posters, packaging and editorial display rooted in the Victorian poster tradition.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe the Italian as marking one of the stranger episodes in the typefounders' experimentation in the early nineteenth century. Reversing the fatface's stress, so thins become thicks and thicks become thins, creates an unusual effect. They note Caslon introduced them in 1821, the punches remain in St Bride library, and unlike other styles the Italian did not attain widescale success.

name: Caslon Italian Shaded
classification_notes: Commercial Type publish Caslon Italian Shaded as a shaded variant within the Caslon Italian programme, the reverse-contrast display style Caslon introduced in 1821.
summary: A shaded cut of Caslon Italian, the reverse-contrast display style introduced by Caslon in 1821.
description: A shaded display cut by Paul Barnes that adds a layered, drop-shadowed treatment to Caslon's 1821 reverse-contrast Italian, where thins become thicks and thicks become thins. Sits within the wider Caslon revival programme alongside Caslon Italian and Caslon French Antique, suited to posters, packaging, signage and editorial display that want a Victorian display register with extra theatrical weight.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type publish Caslon Italian Shaded as part of the Caslon revival programme drawn by Paul Barnes, alongside Caslon Italian, Caslon French Antique and the wider Caslon Doric system.

name: Caslon Rounded
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Caslon Rounded as a headline design rooted in the first rounded typeface, cut by the Caslon & Catherwood foundry in 1836, with matching italic, inline and ornamented variants.
summary: A rounded headline face within the Caslon programme, drawn from the 1836 Caslon & Catherwood original.
description: A rounded headline face by Paul Barnes that revives the first rounded typeface, cut by the Caslon & Catherwood foundry in 1836. Rounding the terminals gives a warm, friendly appearance, and with a well-considered lowercase the design is remade for today. Published with matching italic, inline and ornamented variants for posters, packaging, identity and editorial display where you want nineteenth-century warmth and a clear sense of character.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Caslon Rounded as a design where rounding the terminals of letters has long fascinated designers, citing the first rounded typeface cut by the Caslon & Catherwood foundry in 1836. They note the rounded terminals give it a warm and friendly appearance and, with a well-considered lowercase, it is a design from the past remade for today, with matching italic, inline and ornamented variants.

name: Caslon Rounded Ornamented
classification_notes: Commercial Type publish Caslon Rounded Ornamented as the ornamented variant within the Caslon Rounded family, drawn from the 1836 Caslon & Catherwood rounded original.
summary: The ornamented variant of Caslon Rounded, decorated in the Victorian display tradition for posters and packaging.
description: An ornamented headline face by Paul Barnes that decorates the rounded skeleton of Caslon Rounded with patterned infill in the Victorian display tradition. Sits alongside the standard, italic and inline cuts of Caslon Rounded as part of the broader Caslon revival programme, useful for short bursts of display typography on posters, packaging, signage and editorial spreads that want a warm, decorated nineteenth-century voice.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type publish Caslon Rounded Ornamented as part of the Caslon Rounded family drawn by Paul Barnes, alongside matching italic and inline variants of the 1836 Caslon & Catherwood rounded original.

name: Chiswick Grotesque
classification_notes: Commercial Type place Chiswick Grotesque within the late-1820s and 1830s explosion of sans serifs, framed as the bold, stripped-down letter of nineteenth-century street scenes rather than printing types, with round geometric bowls and an industrial aesthetic.
summary: A vernacular nineteenth-century grotesque from the wider Chiswick programme, drawn from the bold, geometric street letters of the 1830s.
description: A vernacular grotesque by Paul Barnes that captures the bold, stripped-down sans serif letters that exploded across British street scenes in the late 1820s and 1830s. Chiswick Grotesque has the round, geometric bowls and industrial aesthetic of letters cast for machines and factory signage rather than printing types, sitting alongside Chiswick and Chiswick Sans for editorial and identity work that wants a crude, nineteenth-century display voice.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Chiswick Grotesque as the letter we see in street scenes of the nineteenth century: powerful and often cruder in style than printing types, with round geometric bowls exhibiting an industrial aesthetic. They frame it as the letter an engineer would use on a machine, or with which a sign painter would adorn a factory, a letter for casting in a metal foundry rather than a typefoundry.

name: Chiswick Sans
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Chiswick Sans as a high-contrast sans whose origin lies in an unusual lettercut example found in the far west of England, with the contrast of a modern serif but without any serifs.
summary: A high-contrast sans by Paul Barnes that strips the serifs from Chiswick, drawing on an unusual English lettercut find.
description: A high-contrast sans by Paul Barnes that removes the serifs from his Chiswick programme without losing the contrast of a modern serif. Drawn after the discovery of an unusual highly contrasted sans lettercut in the far west of England, the family runs across Poster, standard and Text optical sizes with six weights from Thin to Black and matching italics, suited to fashion, beauty, luxury and editorial work that wants the elegance of Chiswick with a stripped, sans silhouette.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe how, during the long process of designing Chiswick, an unusual example of a highly contrasted sans was discovered in the far west of England with the contrast of a modern style serif but without any serifs. They note that before sans became codified as a genre of typeface, high-contrast sans letters were surprisingly typical, and that Chiswick Sans successfully manages to retain the beauty of Chiswick without feeling like anything is missing.

name: Chiswick Serif Collection
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Chiswick as a seriffed typeface drawn from the British lettering tradition that began in the 18th century, following lettering rather than typographic sources, with a steeply angled italic in the spirit of roundhand copperplate script. Sits between Modern and writing-master lettering, with extensive swashes and alternates.
summary: A warm, lettering-based serif by Paul Barnes drawing on 18th-century British writing masters, with a copperplate italic and four optical sizes.
description: A high-contrast serif by Paul Barnes that follows the British lettering tradition of the 18th and 19th century rather than the Modern typographic sources of the era. The italic carries a steep, copperplate-style angle, and the family ships with an extensive set of swashes, alternates, small capitals and figure styles in both roman and italic. Four optical sizes (Poster, Headline, Deck and Text) cover everything from giant display to running text. Sits in editorial design, social stationery and luxury identity work where a timeless elegance is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Chiswick as a seriffed typeface, warm and elegant, influenced by the British lettering tradition beginning in the 18th century. It follows the lettering tradition rather than typographic sources, from the pen of the writing master to the chisel of the letter cutter, capturing the vernacular seriffed letters of the 18th and 19th century. Released across four families for different sizes, it is positioned for graphic design, social stationery and editorial design where timeless elegance is required.

name: Control Collection
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Control as a sans serif built on Walter Käch's 1949 lettering manual, with parametric axes for weight, contrast and apertures. The Cursive is loosely inspired by Jan van Dijk's 1982 Letraset design, with super-tight TNT versions developed for Interview magazine.
summary: A parametric sans by Schwartz and Reyes built on Walter Kach's 1949 lettering manual, with axes for weight, contrast and apertures, plus a Letraset-flavoured Cursive.
description: A sans serif system by Christian Schwartz and Miguel Reyes based on the lettering examples in Walter Kach's 1949 manual Schriften Lettering Ecriture. Control treats the manual literally: rather than one correct form, the family offers axes for weight, contrast and apertures so designers tune the right combination per use. The Cursive draws loosely on Van Dijk's 1982 Letraset design, and the super-tight TNT cuts were developed for Interview magazine, where the family first appeared. Suits fashion, editorial and identity work that wants modernist rigour with room for high-fashion or downtown character.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Control as designed by Christian Schwartz and Miguel Reyes, using the sans serif lettering examples in Walter Kach's 1949 manual Schriften Lettering Ecriture as a basis. Axes for weight, contrast and apertures let users find the right combination for any application, with no correct version. The Cursive is loosely inspired by Van Dijk and the super-tight TNT cuts were inspired by Interview magazine's archive.

name: Dala Floda Collection
classification_notes: Commercial Type position Dala Floda as a Renaissance-rooted stencil family, with companion Dala Moa as a sans serif stencil and Dala Prisma as an inline display addition. Inspired by worn gravestone lettering and shipping crate stencils.
summary: A Renaissance-rooted stencil serif by Paul Barnes and Ben Kiel, with a sans counterpart Dala Moa and an inline Dala Prisma for very large sizes.
description: A stencil serif by Paul Barnes and Ben Kiel, with Ilya Ruderman, that takes Renaissance letterform structure and applies a stencil cut inspired by worn gravestones and shipping crate lettering. The companion Dala Moa carries the same idea into a sans, and the later Dala Prisma adds dramatic tapering for very large display sizes only. The elegance of the forms belies their everyday vernacular origins, making the family especially well suited to headlines and logotypes.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Dala Floda and Dala Moa as rooted in Renaissance typefaces with the twist of being stencil letterforms, with Dala Moa as the sans serif companion. They were originally inspired by worn gravestone lettering and lettering on shipping crates, the elegance of the forms belying their everyday origins. Dala Prisma is a later addition for very large sizes only, because of its intricate and dramatic tapering of strokes.

name: Darby Sans Collection
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Darby Sans as a humanist sans rooted in the British lettering and typefounding tradition of the mid-18th century, drawing on Baskerville, Joseph Fry and Alexander Wilson. Two related cuts: a functional Darby Sans and a higher-contrast display version.
summary: A humanist sans by Paul Barnes and Dan Milne drawn for Wallpaper magazine, rooted in 18th-century British type founding from Baskerville, Fry and Wilson.
description: A humanist sans by Paul Barnes and Dan Milne originally drawn for Wallpaper magazine, split into a functional Darby Sans for working text and a higher-contrast display cut for large sizes. The skeletons reach back to the British type founding tradition that flowered in the mid-18th century, behind the contemporary curves you can read the structure of Baskerville of Birmingham, Joseph Fry of Bristol and Alexander Wilson of Glasgow. Sits in editorial, graphic, corporate and advertising work that wants a calm British sans with historical depth.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Darby Sans as a contemporary family of two related humanist sans serifs: the functional Darby Sans and a refined display version with dramatically higher contrast. Originally designed for Wallpaper magazine, the roots lie in the British lettering and typefounding tradition of the mid-18th century, with structural qualities drawn from John Baskerville, Joseph Fry and Alexander Wilson.

name: Darby Serif Collection
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Darby Serif as a contemporary serif built on the same skeleton as Darby Sans, with proportions drawn from 20th-century news typefaces and roots in British Enlightenment lettering. Split into Display and Text optical sizes.
summary: A contemporary British transitional serif by Paul Barnes built on the Darby skeleton, with Display and Text optical sizes for editorial and news work.
description: A serif by Paul Barnes with Dan Milne, drawn on the same skeleton as Darby Sans and split into a Display family for headlines and a Text family for continuous reading. Its mixture of roundness and sharpness pulls from the lettering and typefaces of the British Enlightenment, while the proportions follow the functional newsfaces of the 20th century, giving headline and text economy without losing legibility under difficult printing conditions. Suits news, editorial, graphic and corporate design across digital and print.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Darby Serif as a contemporary serif made up of Darby Serif Display for headlines and Darby Serif Text for continuous reading matter. Based on the same skeleton as Darby Sans, it carries rich historical roots in 18th-century British Enlightenment lettering combined with the utilitarian proportions of 20th-century newsfaces, suited to news in digital and print, editorial design, graphic design and corporate design.

name: Delegate Collection
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Delegate as a hardworking sans serif celebrating Bell Gothic and anonymous functional grotesques, with truncated terminals, angled ascenders, stroke contrast and eccentric figures that solve legibility problems while keeping a distinct character.
summary: A hardworking grotesque by Shiva Nallaperumal in the spirit of Bell Gothic, with truncated terminals and angled ascenders for clarity at small sizes.
description: A grotesque sans by Shiva Nallaperumal that draws on Bell Gothic and other anonymous, highly functional grotesques. Truncated terminals keep apertures open, angled ascenders differentiate I from l without serifs, stroke contrast protects counterforms and idiosyncratic figures clearly separate 3 from 8, 6 and 9. The design treats functionalism as both purpose and aesthetic, making it well suited to directories, ui, wayfinding, captions and editorial systems that need to work hard at small sizes.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Delegate as a hardworking sans serif by Shiva Nallaperumal that celebrates Bell Gothic and other anonymous, highly functional grotesques that manage to be invisible despite being made of idiosyncratic shapes. Truncated terminals, angled ascenders, stroke contrast and eccentric figures combine into a typeface that treats functionalism as both purpose and aesthetic.

name: Delusse Family
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Delusse as a Latin text face balancing spikiness and softness, drawing obvious inspiration from Francois Ganeau's Vendome with a gentler tone for longform reading, paired with italics invented by Sandrine Nugue. Named after Marguerite Delusse, an 18th-century French engraver.
summary: A French text serif by Sandrine Nugue with the spikiness of Vendome softened for longform reading, named after the 18th-century engraver Marguerite Delusse.
description: A text serif by Sandrine Nugue with Thomas Bouillet, balancing spikiness and softness. The uprights draw obvious inspiration from Francois Ganeau's boldly graphic Vendome, one of the most iconic French typefaces of the 20th century, but pull the tone back for longform reading. The italics are Nugue's own invention. The family is named after Marguerite Delusse, married name of Marguerite Vendome, one of the most skilled engravers of the late 18th century. Suits literary editorial, fashion and any setting that wants a French serif voice without Vendome's full theatrical contrast.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Delusse as a Latin text face by Sandrine Nugue with assistance from Thomas Bouillet, balancing spikiness and softness. The uprights draw obvious inspiration from Francois Ganeau's boldly graphic Vendome, with a gentler tone more suited to longform reading. The italics could only have been invented by Nugue. The family is named after Marguerite Delusse, the married name of Marguerite Vendome, one of the most skilled engravers of the late 18th century.

name: Dignitas Family
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Dignitas as a crisp and modern interpretation of classical Roman capitals inscribed in stone, with Trajan's Column as exemplar and Aldo Novarese's Nova Augustea as initial reference. Adds a lowercase and stylistic alternates uncommon for the genre.
summary: A crisp modern take on Roman inscriptional capitals by Schwartz and Sanchez, drawing on Trajan's Column and Aldo Novarese's Nova Augustea, with a rare lowercase.
description: A display serif by Christian Schwartz and Dino Sanchez, with Jesse Vega, interpreting the classical capitals inscribed in stone across the Roman empire, exemplified by the inscription below Trajan's Column. Using Aldo Novarese's Nova Augustea as initial inspiration, the designers revisited varied sources to produce a modern reading of the genre. Unusually for a Roman capital revival, Dignitas includes a lowercase alphabet and a surprising range of stylistic variety in a small family. Suits heritage, institutional, luxury and editorial work where a classical capital voice is wanted with contemporary detail.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Dignitas as a crisp and modern interpretation of the classical capitals inscribed in stone throughout the Roman empire, exemplified by the inscription below Trajan's Column in Rome. Designed by Christian Schwartz and Dino Sanchez, with Jesse Vega, using Aldo Novarese's Nova Augustea as initial inspiration. Unique in the genre for offering a lowercase alphabet and a surprising range of stylistic variety in a small family.

name: Druk Collection
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Druk as a study in extremes: the narrowest, widest and heaviest typefaces in the Commercial Type library to date. Built without a normal width and without lighter-than-medium weights, focused on uncompromising bold display use across multiple widths.
summary: Berton Hasebe's extreme display grotesque, drawn only in heavy weights across Condensed, standard and Wide widths for posters, mastheads and campaigns.
description: A heavy display grotesque by Berton Hasebe, with Panagiotis Haratzopoulos and Ilya Ruderman for non-Latin scripts. Druk runs from Medium up to Super only, with no light weights and no normal width, so the family stays focused on uncompromising bold display use. The three widths (Condensed, standard, Wide) plus matching Text cuts can be mixed for bold, expressive typographic layouts. It first appeared as a companion to Neue Haas Grotesk and pairs equally well with Atlas, Graphik and Marr Sans. Reach for it on magazine covers, posters, mastheads and campaign headlines.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Druk as a study in extremes, featuring the narrowest, widest and heaviest typefaces in the Commercial Type library to date. Starting from Medium and going up to Super, Druk is uncompromisingly bold, intentionally designed without a normal width and without lighter-than-medium weights so the focus stays on its most emphatic styles. Designed by Berton Hasebe with Panagiotis Haratzopoulos and Ilya Ruderman, the family ships in Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.

name: Duplicate Collection
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Duplicate as a sans serif system started in 2007 by Christian Schwartz as a deliberate misremembering of Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive, grown in collaboration with Miguel Reyes into five related families: Sans, Slab, Ionic, Soft and Round, plus a Sans Condensed cut.
summary: A multi-genre sans system by Schwartz and Reyes that started as a deliberate misremembering of Antique Olive, grown into Sans, Slab, Ionic, Soft and Round companions.
description: A sans serif system by Christian Schwartz and Miguel Reyes, started in 2007 as Schwartz's experiment in playing Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive from memory, the way musicians stumble on their sound by getting it wrong. The result was a sans with its own personality, grown over time into five related families: Sans, Slab, Ionic, Soft and Round, with a Sans Condensed cut for tighter measures. Suits editorial systems and identity work that wants a single voice flexed across multiple structural moods, from humanist sans to slab and softened script-adjacent forms.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Duplicate as starting in 2007 when Christian Schwartz, inspired by musicians who found their sound by playing pieces they loved from memory and getting them wrong, applied the same process to Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive. The result was a new sans serif with a personality of its own, grown into five related families in collaboration with Miguel Reyes: Sans, Slab, Ionic, Soft and Round.

name: Ergon Family
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Ergon as a sculptural display serif by Hrvoje Zivcic combining stone-chiseled letterforms with sharp digital drawing. Wide weight range pushes the concept to extremes, from wordmark-heavy darks to delicate, weathered-stone lights.
summary: A sculptural incised serif by Hrvoje Zivcic that fuses chiseled stone letterforms with sharp digital drawing across a wide weight range.
description: An incised display serif by Hrvoje Zivcic that pulls stone-chiseled letterforms together with sharp digital drawing to produce sculptural graphic forms. Across a wide weight range the design is pushed to its extremes: darker weights take on a wordmark quality, while lighter cuts feel gentle and evocative of weathered stone inscriptions. Suits identity, luxury, heritage and editorial work that wants distinct, eye-catching typography across print and digital.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Ergon, designed by Hrvoje Zivcic, as artfully combining influences from stone-chiseled letters and sharp digital drawing to produce sculptural graphic forms. The wide range of weights tests and pushes the concept to its extremes, with darker weights possessing a wordmark quality and lighter styles feeling gentle, delicate and evocative of time-worn stone inscriptions.

name: Eugenio Sans Collection
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Eugenio Sans as a geometric sans drawn to complement Eugenio Serif in Francesco Franchi's overhaul of La Repubblica, partially inspired by Massimo Vignelli's Bodoni and Futura combinations, with flat vertical terminals and open apertures tuned for newsprint.
summary: A geometric sans by Greg Gazdowicz drawn for La Repubblica, tuned for newsprint with flat vertical terminals and open apertures.
description: A geometric sans by Greg Gazdowicz, drawn to complement Eugenio Serif in Francesco Franchi's overhaul of La Repubblica. Partially inspired by the classic Bodoni and Futura combinations popularised by Massimo Vignelli, the family serves the paper from big drop caps down to captions, sidebars and infographics. Flat vertical terminals create open apertures and clean spacing between letters, giving the design a straightforward, newsy tone. Splits into a Display family for headlines and a Text family for smaller sizes.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Eugenio Sans, designed by Greg Gazdowicz to complement Eugenio Serif in Francesco Franchi's overhaul of La Repubblica, as a geometric sans that can be at turns stylish and pragmatic. Partially inspired by the classic Bodoni and Futura combination Massimo Vignelli popularised in the 20th century, it serves the paper at many sizes and is the primary headline typeface for Sport and other feature sections, with Eugenio Serif dedicated to news. The terminals end at flat verticals, giving open apertures and a newsy tone.

name: Eugenio Serif Collection
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Eugenio Serif as a softened Bodoni revival drawn by Miguel Reyes with Hrvoje Zivcic for Francesco Franchi's La Repubblica redesign, with rounded corners and gently softened serifs that recall phototype rather than crisp digital forms. Includes a companion Eugenia for very large display.
summary: A softened Bodoni revival by Miguel Reyes drawn for La Repubblica, with rounded corners and phototype warmth across three subfamilies.
description: A modern serif by Miguel Reyes with Hrvoje Zivcic, drawn for Francesco Franchi's redesign of La Repubblica and still the primary headline face after the paper's reworking. Though fairly faithful to Bodoni, the softness of the serifs and subtly rounded corners lend it warmth, recalling phototype rather than crisp digital letterforms. Reyes referenced 16pt and 24pt specimens from a facsimile of Giambattista Bodoni's 1818 Manuale Typographico. Splits into Poster and standard cuts plus a companion Eugenia for very large display sizes.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Eugenio Serif, designed by Miguel Reyes with Hrvoje Zivcic for Francesco Franchi's redesign of La Repubblica, as a fairly faithful rendition of Bodoni in which the softness of the serifs and subtly rounded corners lend warmth, making it feel more like phototype than crisp, perfect digital letterforms. The primary historical sources were 16pt and 24pt specimens in a facsimile of Giambattista Bodoni's 1818 Manuale Typographico.

name: Feature
designer: Christian Schwartz, Berton Hasebe, Hrvoje Zivcic
classification_notes: Commercial Type frames Feature as an editorial contemporary serif first drawn for T: The New York Times Style Magazine, with diagonal stress, mismatched contrast between main strokes and serifs, and sharply angled head serifs giving the face tension and immediacy.
summary: A tense, contemporary editorial serif from Commercial Type, first cut for T Magazine and expanded into a five-width fashion and feature system.
description: A contemporary serif by Christian Schwartz and Berton Hasebe, originally drawn in 2018 for T: The New York Times Style Magazine and later expanded by Hrvoje Zivcic. Diagonal stress, mismatched contrast between main strokes and serifs, and sharply angled head serifs give Feature its sense of tension and dynamism. The collection now runs from Text up through Deck and Display, plus condensed cuts, with matching italics across the whole range and small caps in Text. Reach for it on editorial covers, fashion mastheads and feature openers that want immediacy without slipping into pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Feature as originally designed by Christian Schwartz and Berton Hasebe in 2018 for T: The New York Times Style Magazine, with diagonal stress, mismatched contrast between main strokes and serifs, and sharply angled head serifs giving the face tension, dynamism and immediacy. The collection has been expanded by Hrvoje Zivcic, who broadened the weight range, finished the condensed display cuts and drew italics throughout. The Text cut comes with small caps, and the wider family carries useful alternates.

name: Focal
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Focal as a phototype-era revival rather than a particular face, capturing the warm, slightly degraded quality of 1970s print as hot metal gave way to phototypesetting.
summary: Greg Gazdowicz's revival of the 1970s phototype feel, with a small x-height main cut and a big x-height Maxi companion.
description: A serif family by Greg Gazdowicz that reads as a revival of an era rather than a single face. Focal captures the warm, slightly degraded quality of 1970s print as hot metal gave way to phototypesetting, with a relatively small x-height that bucks recent trends. Focal Maxi flips one of the period's other hallmarks, the giant x-height popularised by ITC and Adrian Frutiger's Vectora, giving you a sibling face built for tighter measures and smaller sizes. Six weights run from Light to Black with italics throughout. Good for editorial brands and books that want familiar warmth without straight pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Focal as Greg Gazdowicz's unusual revival, not of a specific typeface but of the particular quality of 1970s print production, when the hot-metal era gave way to phototypesetting and type as a whole lost some of its sharpness. Its relatively small x-height bucks recent trends, leaving an impression of a familiar but forgotten mid- to late-twentieth century face, xeroxed and copied over until it becomes organic and warm. Focal Maxi applies another seventies hallmark, the giant x-height beloved by ITC and used by Adrian Frutiger on Vectora.

name: Frame
designer: Paul Barnes, Dan Milne, Thomas Bouillet
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Frame as a transitional serif drawing on William Caslon I, in particular his 1734 Great Primer, with Dutch seventeenth-century influence behind the cut.
summary: A sharp Caslon-influenced serif by Paul Barnes, originally drawn for Rapha, with head and text optical sizes plus a variable cut.
description: A transitional serif by Paul Barnes, designed for Rapha and rooted in William Caslon I, particularly his 1734 Great Primer with its Dutch seventeenth-century DNA. The family comes in four weights with separate Head and Text cuts and a variable version with axes for weight and optical size. Frame has a large x-height and reduced descenders, with simple, minimally tapered serifs and a matching sharpness in the ball terminals. The italic carries the steepness and regularity of angle seen in some of Caslon's own work, characterful without being distracting. Barnes was assisted by Dan Milne and Thomas Bouillet.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Frame as drawn by Paul Barnes for Rapha, taking inspiration from William Caslon I, in particular his Great Primer cut in 1734, itself indebted to the Dutch masters of the seventeenth century. The family comes in four weights with separate versions for text and headlines, plus a variable font with axes for weight and optical size. It has a large x-height and reduced descenders, simple serifs with minimal tapering, sharp ball terminals, and an italic with the steepness and regularity of angle seen in some of Caslon's work. Barnes was assisted by Dan Milne and Thomas Bouillet.

name: Giorgio
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Giorgio and Giorgio Sans as a paired serif and sans system drawn for T Magazine, bringing runway proportions to the page in contrasting ways.
summary: Christian Schwartz's slender fashion display family for T Magazine, paired with a matching Giorgio Sans for contrast.
description: A high-contrast fashion serif by Christian Schwartz designed for Chris Martinez at T, the New York Times Style Magazine, with its sibling Giorgio Sans bringing the same runway proportions in stripped-back form. Multiple optical sizes from Small through XLarge let the face hold both editorial body and full-bleed display, and the wide weight range spans Thin to Black with italics throughout. Strong on covers, fashion features and luxury identities that want elongated proportions without losing personality.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Giorgio and its matching sans as designed for Chris Martinez at T, the New York Times Style Magazine, bringing runway proportions to the page in contrasting ways.

name: Goldscheider
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Goldscheider as a titling face drawn loosely from the elegant lettering Ludwig Goldscheider used in early twentieth-century Phaidon monographs.
summary: An elegant set of titling caps by Christian Schwartz, drawn from Ludwig Goldscheider's Phaidon lettering and first used as the masthead of Document Journal.
description: A set of titling caps by Christian Schwartz, drawn loosely from the elegant lettering Ludwig Goldscheider used in the lush but affordable monographs he wrote, edited, designed and published as co-founder of Phaidon in the early twentieth century. Books on Da Vinci, El Greco, Michelangelo and Holbein supplied the main inspiration. Schwartz first drew the face for Nick Vogelson in 2020 as the nameplate for Document Journal, then completed it as a full titling set used by the magazine until it folded in 2026. Suits art-book covers, fashion mastheads and luxury identities wanting period elegance without revival cliche.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Goldscheider as based loosely on the elegant lettering Ludwig Goldscheider used in the lush but affordable monographs he wrote, edited, designed, and published as co-founder of Phaidon in the early twentieth century, with books on Da Vinci, El Greco, Michelangelo and Holbein providing the main inspiration. Christian Schwartz drew this version for Nick Vogelson, first as the 2020 nameplate for Document Journal, then later as a complete set of titling caps, used until the magazine folded in 2026.

name: Graphik
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Graphik as a deliberately vanilla editorial workhorse, a hybrid that offsets the round bowls of a geometric sans with the architecture and proportions of a European grotesk, without the baggage of more dogmatic postwar sans serifs.
summary: Christian Schwartz's deliberately vanilla editorial sans, now a sprawling system across nine weights and eight widths with a slab sibling in Produkt.
description: A neo-grotesque sans by Christian Schwartz, deliberately vanilla and built as an editorial workhorse. Graphik offsets the round bowls typical of a geometric sans with the architecture and proportions of a European grotesk, skipping the baggage of more familiar postwar faces. Low contrast, open counters and compact descenders make it work in tight settings like subheads, captions and labels, while round dots and other human touches keep it warm at title and poster sizes. The family has matured into nine weights and eight widths, from Compact through five degrees of Condensed and out to Wide, and has even spawned a slab companion in Produkt. Remains one of the most-used sans serifs in editorial and brand work.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Graphik as a deliberately vanilla editorial workhorse and one of their most popular families. A hybrid face that offsets the round bowls typical of a geometric sans with the architecture and proportions of a European grotesk, without the baggage of more dogmatic, overused sans serifs mired in postwar modernism. Graphik's low contrast, open counters and compact descenders recommend it for subheads, captions, labels and running text, while human touches like round dots and other details suit larger uses such as titles, covers, posters and signage. Over time, the family has matured into nine weights and eight widths, and has branched out into a companion slab family, Produkt.

name: Graphik Arabic
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Graphik Arabic as a Wael Morcos and Khajag Apelian adaptation that combines the simplified strokes of a grotesque with the structure and proportion of a fluid script.
summary: Wael Morcos and Khajag Apelian's Arabic companion to Graphik, leaning into utility rather than calligraphic detail.
description: An Arabic companion to Graphik by Wael Morcos and Khajag Apelian. Where the Latin Graphik straddles the line between geometric sans and European grotesk, the Arabic combines a grotesque's simplified strokes with the structure and proportion of a fluid script. The aim is a meaningful departure from calligraphic detailing, presenting a utilitarian workhorse with a plain style. Nine weights from Thin to Super track the Latin master, making the pair safe for editorial systems and brands that work across both scripts.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Graphik, designed by Christian Schwartz and released in 2009, as a reinterpretation of lesser-known mid-century types unhampered by the baggage of more familiar faces like Univers, Helvetica or Futura, straddling the round bowls of a geometric sans and the structures and proportions of a European grotesk. Graphik Arabic, designed by Wael Morcos and Khajag Apelian, combines the simplified strokes of a grotesque with the structure and proportion of a fluid script, departing from calligraphic detailing to present a utilitarian workhorse with a plain style.

name: Graphik Armenian
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Graphik Armenian as Khajag Apelian's adaptation of Graphik's vanilla concept to Armenian, with simplified stroke endings and contextual alternates to keep the script working at the same plain register as the Latin.
summary: Khajag Apelian's Armenian companion to Graphik, with reduced x-height and contextual alternates tuned to the script.
description: An Armenian companion to Graphik by Khajag Apelian. The original concept of a blank-slate, vanilla sans is translated to Armenian with different vertical proportions to the Latin, reducing the x-height and lengthening descenders so the script feels balanced both on its own and in multilingual settings. Stroke endings and letter elements traditionally considered fundamental on letters like Za, Eh and Jheh have been simplified, with contextual alternates handling the trade-off between traditional structure and distracting collisions. Nine weights with italics make it a strong pick for editorial systems and brands working across Armenian and Latin.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Graphik Armenian as designed to be a blank slate, with Khajag Apelian translating Graphik's vanilla concept to Armenian. The adaptation uses different vertical proportions to the original Latin, with reduced x-height and longer descenders so Armenian text looks balanced on its own and in multilingual applications. Stroke endings and letter additions classically considered fundamental in letters such as Za, Eh and Jheh were simplified and in some cases removed, with contextual alternates helping to split the difference between traditional structures and distracting collisions between letterforms.

name: Graphik Bangla
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Graphik Bangla as an Arya Purohit adaptation of the Graphik concept to the Bengali script, with creative direction by Shiva Nallaperumal at November, keeping the ball terminals that make Bengali legible.
summary: Arya Purohit's Bengali companion to Graphik, retaining the script's ball terminals for legibility.
description: A Bengali companion to Graphik by Arya Purohit, with creative direction from Shiva Nallaperumal at November. The adaptation carries Graphik's plain, vanilla character into the Bengali script while keeping the ball terminals that make Bangla read clearly. Nine weights from Thin to Super match the Latin master across editorial, branding and publishing applications that need to work in both scripts.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Graphik Bangla as Arya Purohit's adaptation of the Graphik concept to Bengali, designed with creative direction from Shiva Nallaperumal at November. They explain that the Latin Graphik, released in 2009, was inspired by expressive graphic design made with plain, vanilla typefaces and reinterpreted lesser-known mid-century types unhampered by the baggage of faces like Univers, Helvetica or Futura. The Bangla extends this plainness to the Bengali script while keeping the ball terminals that make the script legible.

name: Graphik Devanagari
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Graphik Devanagari as Hitesh Malaviya's adaptation of the Graphik concept to Devanagari, with creative direction by Shiva Nallaperumal at November, using generous proportions and unadorned shapes for an open, optimistic feel.
summary: Hitesh Malaviya's Devanagari companion to Graphik, open and optimistic for editorial and brand work.
description: A Devanagari companion to Graphik by Hitesh (Rocky) Malaviya, with creative direction from Shiva Nallaperumal at November. foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Graphik Devanagari as Hitesh Malaviya's adaptation of Graphik to the Devanagari script, with creative direction from Shiva Nallaperumal at November. They explain that the Latin Graphik, released in 2009, was inspired by expressive graphic design made with plain, vanilla typefaces and reinterpreted lesser-known mid-century types unhampered by the baggage of faces like Univers, Helvetica or Futura. The Devanagari extends this plainness with generous proportions and unadorned shapes for legibility and an optimistic openness.

name: Graphik Georgian
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Graphik Georgian as Yury Ostromentsky's adaptation, simplifying structures that are typically more complex in Georgian for a smooth and even tone.
summary: Yury Ostromentsky's Georgian companion to Graphik, smoothing complex structures into a steady, even tone.
description: A Georgian companion to Graphik by Yury Ostromentsky, simplifying structures that are typically more complex in Georgian for a smoother, more even tone in running text. Nine weights with matching italics sit alongside the Latin Graphik, giving editorial brands and publications a calm, plain sans across the two scripts.
