name: Graphik Hebrew
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Graphik Hebrew as Yanek Iontef and Daniel Grumer's extension of Graphik's vanilla concept to Hebrew, a script with no equivalent grotesk genre, using stripped-down construction with sharp corners that echo characters like Z and 2.
summary: Yanek Iontef and Daniel Grumer's Hebrew companion to Graphik, translating the vanilla concept into a script with no native grotesk tradition.
description: A Hebrew companion to Graphik by Yanek Iontef and Daniel Grumer. Hebrew has no equivalent to the grotesk genre, so the designers took Graphik's stripped-down, plain approach and added the sharp corners seen on characters like Z and 2 to give the script its own texture. Nine weights with matching italics line up with the Latin Graphik, suiting editorial brands and publications working across Hebrew and Latin.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Graphik Hebrew as an extension of Graphik's vanilla concept to Hebrew, a script with no equivalent to the grotesk genre. They use Graphik's stripped-down and straightforward approach to construction and bring in the sharp corners seen on characters like Z and 2 to provide texture. They note that the Latin Graphik, released in 2009, was inspired by expressive graphic design made with plain vanilla typefaces and reinterpreted lesser-known mid-century types unhampered by the baggage of faces like Univers, Helvetica or Futura.

name: Graphik International
designer: Christian Schwartz, Khajag Apelian, Wael Morcos, Arya Purohit, Hitesh Malaviya, Yury Ostromentsky, Yanek Iontef, Daniel Grumer, Smich Smanloh, Knaz Uiyamathiti, Anuthin Wongsunkakon
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Graphik International as the umbrella pack collecting all script adaptations of Graphik, from Latin through Arabic, Armenian, Bangla, Devanagari, Georgian, Hebrew, Tamil and the two Thai cuts.
summary: The umbrella Graphik pack, gathering every script adaptation into one global-brand system across Latin, Arabic, Armenian, Bangla, Devanagari, Georgian, Hebrew, Tamil and Thai.
description: The global edition of Graphik, bundling every script companion into one system: Latin, Arabic, Armenian, Bangla, Devanagari, Georgian, Hebrew, Tamil and two Thai cuts, a loopless modern style and a traditional looped style. Each cut was designed by a script specialist working from Christian Schwartz's original vanilla concept, with nine weights from Thin to Super and matching italics where each script allows. Useful where a single brand or publisher needs a consistent voice across the bulk of the world's reading population.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Graphik, designed by Christian Schwartz and released in 2009, as a reinterpretation of lesser-known mid-century types without the baggage of more familiar ones like Univers, Helvetica or Futura, straddling the round bowls of a geometric sans and the structures and proportions of a European grotesk. Graphik International gathers the script adaptations together; Graphik Thai, designed by Bangkok studio Cadson Demak, interprets the concept in two ways, a sleek modern loopless style that captures Graphik's plain simplicity, and a version with traditional loop terminals.

name: Graphik Tamil
classification_notes: Commercial Type position Graphik Tamil as a Tamil companion to Christian Schwartz's Graphik (2009), a sans inspired by lesser-known mid-century types that sits between geometric round bowls and European grotesk proportions. Hitesh (Rocky) Malaviya designed the Tamil with creative direction from Shiva Nallaperumal at November.
summary: A Tamil companion to Graphik, translating Schwartz's mid-century sans into a calm, plain Tamil for editorial and brand work.
description: A Tamil extension of Commercial Type's Graphik system, designed by Hitesh (Rocky) Malaviya with creative direction from Shiva Nallaperumal at November. Nine weights run from Thin and Extralight up to Black and Super alongside a matching Latin, giving designers the same plain, mid-century voice across scripts. Suits global identity, editorial and publishing systems where Tamil needs to sit confidently next to Graphik in Latin.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Graphik, designed by Christian Schwartz and released in 2009, as a reinterpretation of lesser-known mid-century types that straddles geometric round bowls and European grotesk proportions. Graphik Tamil, drawn by Hitesh (Rocky) Malaviya with creative direction from Shiva Nallaperumal at November, adapts that plainness to the Tamil script.

name: Guardian Egyptian
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Guardian Egyptian as the Egyptian half of the Guardian newspaper system designed for Mark Porter, with a Headline Egyptian and a Text Egyptian sitting alongside Guardian Sans and Guardian Agate.
summary: The Egyptian half of the Guardian newspaper system, an editorial slab in Headline and Text optical sizes built for serious news work.
description: An editorial slab serif designed by Paul Barnes and Christian Schwartz for Mark Porter's redesign of the Guardian newspaper, with help from Panagiotis Haratzopoulos and Ilya Ruderman on the Greek and Cyrillic. Guardian Egyptian sits in two optical sizes, Headline and Text, with weights running from Hairline through to Black with matching italics. Built to cover everything from serious news headlines to expressive features and long-form text, and pairs natively with Guardian Sans and Guardian Agate.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Guardian Egyptian as the result of trying to make a new sans serif by chopping the slabs off an Egyptian, designed for Mark Porter and the Guardian newspaper. The wider system spans an Egyptian for headlines, a Text Egyptian, a Sans for headlines in four widths and an Agate Sans, covering every typographic need of a daily paper.

name: Guardian Sans
classification_notes: Commercial Type position Guardian Sans as the sans half of the Guardian newspaper system, descended from an Egyptian skeleton. The family covers Headline widths from Condensed to standard, Text, and an Agate Sans, with a Duplex pairing for tabular and listings use.
summary: The sans half of the Guardian newspaper system, with Headline, Condensed Headline and Agate widths covering everything from features to financial listings.
description: An editorial sans serif designed by Paul Barnes and Christian Schwartz for Mark Porter's Guardian redesign, with Berton Hasebe and Vincent Chan on the Agate and Panagiotis Haratzopoulos and Ilya Ruderman on the Greek and Cyrillic. Guardian Sans spans Headline and Headline Condensed widths for display, plus Agate Sans and Agate Duplex for tabular and financial settings, with weights from Hairline up to Ultra and matching italics. Built as a system alongside Guardian Egyptian, it covers serious news headlines, features, long-form text and tightly typeset listings.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe the Guardian system as a modern classic born from chopping the slabs off an Egyptian, designed for Mark Porter and the Guardian newspaper. The family covers an Egyptian for headlines, a Text Egyptian, a Sans for headlines in four widths and an Agate Sans, so every typographic need of a daily paper, from serious news to tiny financial listings, can be handled with ease.

name: Ionic Modern
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Ionic Modern as a thread between the robust mid-nineteenth-century Ionic introduced by Caslon and the highly contrasted Modern style that evolved from softer eighteenth-century Moderns, arranged along axes of contrast and weight across Text, Deck and Poster sizes.
summary: A serif system that travels from a sturdy Ionic text face to a highly modulated Modern headline, organised along axes of contrast and weight.
description: A serif system from Commercial Type that joins two nineteenth-century strands, Caslon's robust Ionic and the high-contrast Modern that descended from softer eighteenth-century Moderns. Three optical sizes, Text, Deck and Poster, span six weights from Thin to Extrabold with matching italics. Turning the contrast up takes the workhorse Ionic into subtly contrasted Modern for body copy and finally to a highly modulated headline cut, making it useful across long-form editorial, mastheads and posters.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Ionic Modern as a single family threading together two sources from the second half of the nineteenth century: the robust Ionic style introduced by Caslon and the highly contrasted Modern that evolved from softer, rounder Moderns of the late eighteenth century. Organised along the axes of contrast and weight, the family travels from a workhorse Ionic to a subtly contrasted Modern for text and on to a highly modulated headline style.

name: Isambard
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Isambard as a fat face revival by Paul Barnes, drawing on the boldest of the nineteenth-century Moderns with exaggerated high contrast, inflated ball terminals and characterful swash capitals.
summary: A nineteenth-century fat face revived by Paul Barnes, with exaggerated contrast, ball terminals and joyful swash italics across Text, standard and Condensed cuts.
description: A fat face display serif designed by Paul Barnes with Tim Ripper and Miguel Reyes. Isambard pushes the Modern serif to its inkiest extreme, with high contrast, bulbous ball terminals and a swash-laden italic. The family covers Text, standard, No 2, Condensed and X Condensed widths, each with Roman and Italic, so it can stretch from a smaller editorial setting to maximally compressed posters. Reach for it where you want nineteenth-century theatrical impact rather than a quiet Modern.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe the boldest Moderns as fat faces, pushing the serif letterform to its extremes with exaggerated high contrast and inflated ball terminals to put as much ink on a page as possible. They frame Isambard as continuing that emphatic and joyful tradition, with bulbous swash capitals and a wonderfully characterful italic.

name: Jargon
classification_notes: Commercial Type position Jargon as a typeface shaped by Dutch newspaper types and the work of Francois Ganeau and Roger Excoffon, completed in early 2025 and grown from a single condensed style Thomas Bouillet drew during his Type]Media degree in 2020.
summary: A humanist sans from Thomas Bouillet that channels Dutch newspaper types alongside Ganeau and Excoffon, with six weights and matching italics.
description: A humanist sans designed by Thomas Bouillet for Commercial Type, completed at the start of 2025 and developed from a single condensed style drawn for his Type]Media degree project in 2020. Six weights run from Thin and Light up to Bold, Black and Ultra with matching italics. Jargon blends Dutch newspaper rationality with the warmer French character of Francois Ganeau and Roger Excoffon, sitting comfortably in editorial, brand and publishing work with a defined voice.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Jargon as a typeface that shows the influence of both Dutch newspaper types and the work of Francois Ganeau and Roger Excoffon. They note it was completed at the beginning of 2025 and grew out of a single condensed style from Thomas Bouillet's degree project at Type]Media in the Netherlands in 2020.

name: Le Jeune
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Le Jeune as a contemporary French Modern in the Didot tradition, designed by Paul Barnes and Christian Schwartz for Chris Dixon's refresh of Vanity Fair, with sharp British and American ball terminals rather than soft French teardrops, across four optical sizes.
summary: A modern Didone for fashion and editorial, in four optical sizes from text up to poster, with a stencil variant inspired by French shop lettering.
description: A Modern serif designed by Paul Barnes and Christian Schwartz for Chris Dixon's redesign of Vanity Fair, with Greg Gazdowicz on the stencil cuts. Le Jeune updates the French Modern Didone tradition with sharper, rounder ball terminals borrowed from British and American Moderns. Four optical sizes, Text, Deck, Poster and Hairline, span up to six weights from Light to Black with matching italics, alongside a stencil cut at each size inspired by classic French shop lettering. The default home is fashion, beauty and high-end editorial mastheads.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Le Jeune as a modern adaptation of the French Modern of the nineteenth century, often called Didot, designed by partners Paul Barnes and Christian Schwartz for Chris Dixon's refresh of Vanity Fair. The family features sharp, round ball terminals more typical of British and American Moderns, and comes in four optical sizes from 6 point text up to huge headlines, with a stencil version in each optical size by Greg Gazdowicz inspired by French Modern-style stencil letters.

name: Lyon
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Lyon as Kai Bernau's contemporary interpretation of Robert Granjon's influential sixteenth-century serif typefaces, first released as a text face and later extended with a Display optical size.
summary: Kai Bernau's contemporary take on Granjon's Renaissance serifs, with Text and Display optical sizes for books, editorial and elegant brand work.
description: A contemporary Garalde by Kai Bernau, with Ilya Ruderman and Irene Vlachou on the Cyrillic and Greek. Lyon reinterprets Robert Granjon's sixteenth-century French Renaissance serifs in a calm, modern voice. First released as a text face, the family was later extended with a Display optical size for headline work. Weights run from Light through to Black with matching italics, including a Lyon Text Regular No 2 cut for tighter text settings. Sits in book design, long-form editorial and luxury brand identity work where you want a quiet, classical seriousness.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Lyon as a contemporary interpretation of Robert Granjon's influential sixteenth-century serif typefaces, bringing book and type designer Kai Bernau's fresh point of view to classical Renaissance forms. They note it was first released as a standalone text face and later expanded with an elegant display size.

name: Lyon Arabic
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Lyon Arabic as a Naskh-based companion to Kai Bernau's Lyon, with a Slanted cut drawn from Nastaliq for textural contrast, designed by Khajag Apelian and Wael Morcos.
summary: An Arabic companion to Lyon by Apelian and Morcos, pairing a Naskh upright with a Nastaliq-inspired Slanted cut for editorial and book work.
description: An Arabic companion to Kai Bernau's Lyon, designed by Khajag Apelian and Wael Morcos. The Text cut translates Naskh script characteristics into a typographic register for long passages, with simplified constructions and a robust, slightly digital feel that holds up at small sizes. The Slanted cut uses Nastaliq as a model to bring fluidity and angle for emphasis or contrast. Each axis covers Text and Display optical sizes with weights from Light to Black, and the two upright and slanted families can mix in line or set in parallel for differentiation.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Lyon Arabic Text as a Naskh-based companion to Kai Bernau's original Lyon, designed by Khajag Apelian and Wael Morcos for long passages of text in books and editorial design. They describe Lyon Arabic Slanted as conveying a different texture using the Nastaliq script as a model to bring fluidity and angle, with the two families used on their own, for content differentiation within one layout, or even mixed for in-line emphasis.

name: Marian
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Marian as Paul Barnes's reduction of canonical historical serifs (Garamond, Granjon, van den Keere, Kis, Fleischmann, Fournier, Baskerville, Bodoni, Austin) plus a blackletter, stripped to their skeletal structure as a hairline display family, with a Text companion in some cuts.
summary: A hairline serif system from Paul Barnes that reduces nine canonical serif revivals plus a blackletter to their skeletal structure for display use.
description: A display serif system designed by Paul Barnes with Sandra Carrera and Miguel Reyes. Marian imagines the typographic canon, from Garamond, Granjon, van den Keere and Kis through Fleischmann, Fournier, Baskerville, Bodoni and Austin, plus a blackletter, restyled as hairline outlines. Each style sits at a particular historical date (1554, 1565, 1571, 1680, 1740, 1742, 1757, 1800, 1812) in Roman and Italic, with a Marian Black weight and a Marian Text family for body copy in the 1554, 1757, 1800 and 1812 designs. Sits in fashion, luxury, gallery and editorial design where you want the spirit of a classical model at the lightest possible weight.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Marian as the classics of the typographic canon reinvented for the contemporary world, with Garamond, Granjon, van den Keere, Kis, Fleischmann, Fournier, Baskerville, Bodoni and Austin restyled and revived. They note that these are not strict revivals or interpretations but reductions to basic skeletal forms, with a collection of nine serif typefaces and one blackletter, stripped to their core while retaining the life and spirit of the letters.

name: Marr Sans
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Marr Sans as a characterful grotesque inspired by an 1870s typeface from James Marr & Co. of Edinburgh, expanded by Paul Barnes and Dave Foster from Thin to Bold plus an Ultra Black, with a Condensed companion.
summary: A characterful nineteenth-century Scottish grotesque revived and expanded by Paul Barnes and Dave Foster, with standard and Condensed widths.
description: A grotesque sans serif designed by Paul Barnes and Dave Foster from an 1870s Scottish typeface by James Marr & Co. of Edinburgh, the successors to Alexander Wilson & Sons. Marr Sans expands a handful of original sizes in a single weight into a system running from Thin to Bold with matching italics, plus a wider Ultra Black, and a Condensed cut at the same weights. Where Graphik and Atlas read as twentieth-century neutrals, Marr revels in nineteenth-century individuality and reads as a sharper, more British cousin to Franklin and News Gothic. Suits identity, editorial and packaging that wants distinction without theatre.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type position Marr Sans as a characterful grotesque inspired by a typeface from the 1870s found in the work of James Marr & Co. in Edinburgh, successors to Alexander Wilson & Sons. From a few lines in three sizes, and only one weight, Paul Barnes and Dave Foster expanded the family from Thin to Bold plus an Ultra Black weight, a wider companion to the six lighter weights. They contrast it with the homogeneity of twentieth-century sans serifs and describe it as an eccentric British uncle to Morris Fuller Benton's Franklin and News Gothics.

name: Moulin
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Moulin as an asymmetrical flared serif by Sandrine Nugue, influenced by classical lettering and Adrian Frutiger's underappreciated Icone from 1980, with a sculptural rather than calligraphic character.
summary: An asymmetrical flared serif from Sandrine Nugue with a sculptural rhythm, drawn after Frutiger's Icone and classical lettering.
description: A flared serif designed by Sandrine Nugue for Commercial Type, with an asymmetrical drawing and a distinctive rhythm rooted in classical lettering and Adrian Frutiger's Icone from 1980. Seven weights run from Thin and Light up to Bold, Black and Super with matching italics, holding a comfortable reading texture at small sizes and an assertive personality at display. The balance of black and white and the tension between inside and outside contours gives Moulin a sculptural rather than a calligraphic feel, useful in editorial, identity and packaging work that wants warmth without going fully humanist serif.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Moulin as designed by Sandrine Nugue, an asymmetrical flared design with a distinctive rhythm, influenced by classical lettering and Adrian Frutiger's underappreciated Icone from 1980. They note that the balance of black and white and the tension between inner and outer forms yield a sculptural rather than a calligraphic character, with a comfortable texture at smaller sizes and an assertive personality at display sizes.

name: Neue Haas Grotesk
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Neue Haas Grotesk as Christian Schwartz and Berton Hasebe's restoration of Max Miedinger's original metal Neue Haas Grotesk, produced for Richard Turley's 2010 redesign of Bloomberg Businessweek, with Display and Text optical sizes faithful to Miedinger's shapes and spacing rather than later Helvetica revisions.
summary: A faithful restoration of Miedinger's original Neue Haas Grotesk in Display and Text optical sizes, drawn for Bloomberg Businessweek in 2010.
description: A faithful restoration of Max Miedinger's original Neue Haas Grotesk by Christian Schwartz and Berton Hasebe, completed for Richard Turley's 2010 redesign of Bloomberg Businessweek. Rather than reworking Helvetica, the brief was to bring Miedinger's metal shapes and spacing back to life. The family covers two optical sizes, Display from XXThin up to Black and Text from Roman to Bold, with matching italics and access to Miedinger's original alternates including the straight-legged R and a comma-like cedilla. Sits comfortably in editorial, identity and product work where you want Helvetica's voice closer to its first state.
foundry_blurb: Commercial Type describe Neue Haas Grotesk as a restoration project completed for Richard Turley's 2010 redesign of Bloomberg Businessweek, bringing Miedinger's original Neue Haas Grotesk back to life with as much fidelity to his original shapes and spacing as possible, rather than trying to rethink Helvetica or improve on current digital versions. They note Miedinger's original included interesting alternates such as a cedilla that looked like a flattened comma and a striking straight-legged R, now available in digital form.

name: Nicola
classification_notes: Commercial Type present Nicola as a robust, low-contrast take on Nicolas Jenson's late fifteenth-century Venetian types, filtered through Tommy Thompson's broad-tipped pencil renderings of Caslon from his 1946 manual How to Render Roman Letter Forms. The simplified details verge on slab serif.
summary: A robust, low-contrast Venetian serif from Miguel Reyes that channels Tommy Thompson's pencil renderings of Caslon back through the types of Nicolas Jenson.
description: A two-width family from Miguel Reyes that interprets the late fifteenth-century types of Nicolas Jenson through the hand-rendered Caslon studies in Tommy Thompson's 1946 manual How to Render Roman Letter Forms. Nicola Display covers fourteen styles from Thin to Black with matching italics, and Nicola adds a text companion with Regular, Regular No 2, Medium and Bold plus italics, giving editorial and identity systems an elegant but brash voice with simplified, almost slab-leaning details.
foundry_blurb: Before digital typesetting let designers set their own type, comping (hand-rendering headlines in various typefaces for copyfit and client approval) was an important skill. Miguel Reyes came across Tommy Thompson's classic 1946 manual How to Render Roman Letter Forms and was struck by his renditions of Caslon, written quickly with a broad-tipped pencil. Reyes thought that the tension between the rough, informal quality of the rendering and the classical letterforms would be a fresh way to interpret the types cut by Nicolas Jenson in Venice in the late fifteenth century. Elegant and brash, Nicola is a robust, low-contrast typeface whose simplified details verge on making it a slab serif.

name: Orientation
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Orientation as a geometric sans originally commissioned as a stencil wayfinding system for a student dormitory in Roubaix, France, with characters that become more abstract as weight increases.
summary: A geometric sans by Sandrine Nugue that started life as a stencil wayfinding system for a student dormitory in Roubaix and grew into three weights with italics.
description: A geometric stencil sans by Sandrine Nugue, originally commissioned by Paris designer Thanh Phong Le to be fabricated as wayfinding stencils for a new student dormitory in Roubaix. Six styles cover Light, Regular and Bold with matching italics. As the weight progresses, the forms become more abstract and push the bounds of legibility, but read surprisingly well in running words. Suits architectural, environmental and display work that wants a constructed, fabricated voice.
foundry_blurb: It's common for a typeface to be used for applications beyond the screen and printed page, but far less common for it to start out destined for a different medium. Sandrine Nugue's Orientation was originally commissioned by Paris-based designer Thanh Phong Le, with the simple brief of creating a geometric typeface that could be fabricated as stencils for the wayfinding system of a new student dormitory in Roubaix, France. As the weight range progresses from light to bold, the characters become increasingly abstract, even pushing the bounds of legibility; in the context of words, though, the forms are surprisingly readable.

name: Original Sans
classification_notes: Commercial Type root Original Sans in the earliest sans serif specimens of the Caslon foundry and the Figgins Typefoundry's 1832 capitals, embracing the slightly crude, characterful quality of nineteenth-century display sans serifs.
summary: A revival by Paul Barnes and Christian Schwartz of the earliest sans serifs from Caslon and the Figgins Typefoundry, leaning into the crude, characterful quality of nineteenth-century display sans.
description: A revival sans drawn by Paul Barnes with Christian Schwartz, returning to the earliest specimens of the sans serif in Caslon's books and to a Figgins Typefoundry specimen from 1832. Original Sans is offered in two related cuts, Three and Four, each with italics, capturing the characterful awkwardness of the original capitals-only display sans before lower-case variants were drawn. Best for editorial and identity work that wants a sans with documentary weight and pre-modern texture.
foundry_blurb: The sans serif that had first appeared in the pages of Caslon's specimen books was not immediately popular, and lower case variants did not appear until 18 years after the upper case variant. The pages of the nineteenth century specimens tend to be awash with these slightly crude, yet very characterful capital only variants. This face, from the Figgins Typefoundry, appeared in 1832.

name: Orleans
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Orleans as a crisp display sans drawing on broad-nib calligraphic traditions, including blackletter, and the wider tradition of calligraphic sans serifs. Originally drawn for a redesign of the German edition of frieze.
summary: A crisp seven-weight calligraphic display sans by Paul Barnes, originally drawn for the German edition of frieze and released through the Commercial Type Vault.
description: A display sans drawn by Paul Barnes in 2016, with broad-nib calligraphic logic running through its skeleton. Fourteen styles span Thin, Light, Roman, Medium, Bold, Black and Fat with matching italics, plus a variable axis. Drawn originally for a planned redesign of the German edition of frieze, then developed with Tim Ripper and released through the Commercial Type Vault before its full release in late 2021. Suits magazine mastheads, fashion and cultural identity work.
foundry_blurb: Designed in 2016 by Paul Barnes, Orleans is a crisp sans serif display face that explores the handmade traditions in letterforms created with broad nibs, such as blackletter, but also draws upon the rich tradition of calligraphic sans. Originally drawn for the redesign of the German edition of frieze, the magazine sadly closed before the redesign was implemented. The typeface was further developed with the help of Tim Ripper into seven weights with accompanying italics, first debuting in the Commercial Type Vault before its full release in late 2021.

name: Place
classification_notes: Commercial Type frame Place as a wide display typeface focused on beauty and elegance, with both roman and blackletter capitals and a particular talent for setting logotypes. Drawn by French designer Julien Priez against an open-ended brief.
summary: A wide display serif by Julien Priez with roman and blackletter capitals, drawn for beauty, elegance and logotype work.
description: A two-width family by French designer Julien Priez. Place Display is a wide display serif with both roman and blackletter capitals, drawn for logotypes and luxurious setting at large sizes, while Place adds Light, Regular, Bold and Black with matching italics for editorial use, plus Extra alternates. Suits fashion, beauty and cultural work where a serif needs to feel inventive without losing classical poise.
foundry_blurb: For our first release by French designer Julien Priez, we asked him to draw a wide serif typeface focusing on beauty and elegance. We thought this open-ended brief would perfectly suit his ability to look forward and create wildly inventive shapes while tapping into his deep historical knowledge of drawn, carved, and written letterforms. The result is Place, a display typeface for all sizes with both roman and blackletter capitals, and a particular talent for setting logotypes.

name: Platform
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Platform as an exploration of how the twentieth-century geometric sans can be approached contemporarily, leaning into the inherent crudeness of letters reduced to their simplest essence rather than chasing geometric perfection.
summary: Berton Hasebe's first Commercial Type release, a geometric sans that plays with the crudeness of simplified letterforms rather than chasing perfection.
description: A geometric sans by Berton Hasebe, drawn with Ilya Ruderman for Cyrillic support, and Hasebe's first release for Commercial Type. Ten styles run from Hairline through Bold with matching italics. Where most geometric sans pursue perfection, Platform leans into the inherent crudeness of letters reduced to their simplest essence, distilling ideas from contemporary graphic design into typographic forms. Suits identity, editorial and packaging work that wants a clean, contemporary geometric voice.
foundry_blurb: Platform was the first typeface released by Berton Hasebe. At heart, this family is an exploration of how the geometric sans serif (some of the most well-trod territory of 20th century type design) can be approached in a contemporary context. Rather than aiming for perfection, Platform instead plays with the inherent crudeness in letters that have been reduced to their simplest essence, distilling ideas from contemporary graphic design into typographic forms.

name: Portrait
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Portrait as Berton Hasebe's sharply minimalist depiction of French Renaissance types, marrying classical proportions with triangular Latin serifs across display, text, condensed and inline cuts.
summary: Berton Hasebe's sharply minimalist take on French Renaissance types, with display, text, condensed and inline cuts covering everything from sober editorial to fashion masthead.
description: A four-family system by Berton Hasebe, drawn with Ilya Ruderman for Cyrillic. Portrait covers Light through Heavy with italics for display use, Portrait Text adds a robust workhorse for body, Portrait Condensed extends to five weights for tighter measures, and Portrait Inline brings a decorative cut. The collection marries classical French Renaissance proportions with triangular Latin serifs, ranging from the sober beauty of the display face to the warm sparkle of the text and the exuberance of the condensed and inline cuts. Suits fashion, beauty, cultural and editorial systems.
foundry_blurb: Portrait is Berton Hasebe's sharply minimalist depiction of French Renaissance types. The marriage of classical proportions with triangular Latin serifs is an inspired combination, and a fresh way to see these nearly 500-year-old forms. Of the varied interpretations in the library, the Portrait collection of four families is the most unabashedly new, aggressive in its simplicity but nuanced in its details. It covers a wide range of tones, from the sober beauty of the display face to the warm sparkle of Portrait Text to the exuberance of the Condensed and Inline.

name: Produkt
designer: Christian Schwartz, Berton Hasebe with Ilya Ruderman
classification_notes: Commercial Type position Produkt as the slab serif companion to Graphik, in the tradition of adding slabs to European Grotesks. The serifs are short, particularly in the heaviest weights, keeping Graphik's compact proportions and regular texture.
summary: The slab serif companion to Graphik by Christian Schwartz and Berton Hasebe, with short serifs and a full nine-weight range from Thin to Super.
description: A nine-weight slab serif drawn by Berton Hasebe with Christian Schwartz and Ilya Ruderman, sitting in the European Grotesk slab tradition as a direct companion to Graphik. Eighteen styles run from Thin through Super with matching italics, keeping Graphik's compact proportions and regular texture, with short serifs that grow shorter still in the heaviest cuts. Light weights are pretty, middle weights functional, and heavy weights authoritative. Suits identity, editorial and packaging systems that already use Graphik.
foundry_blurb: Drawn by Berton Hasebe, Produkt is the slab serif companion to Graphik, falling into the tradition of adding slabs to European Grotesk, creating an attractive and functional companion serif typeface. Its serifs are relatively short, particularly in the heaviest weights, so it retains the compact proportions and regular texture that characterize Graphik. The light weights are pretty, the middle weights are functional, and the heavy weights have a feeling of authority, all suffused with the appealing geometry seen in Graphik.

name: Publico
designer: Christian Schwartz, Paul Barnes with Panagiotis Haratzopoulos, Ilya Ruderman, Ross Milne, Thomas Bouillet, Kai Bernau, Andree Paat, Greg Gazdowicz
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Publico as originating en route to The Guardian's 2005 redesign, originally named Hacienda after the Manchester nightclub, then finished for Mark Porter and Simon Esterson's redesign of Publico in Lisbon. It shares the skeleton of the Guardian typeface and acts as its serif companion.
summary: A vast newspaper serif system by Christian Schwartz and Paul Barnes, conceived alongside The Guardian's 2005 redesign and finished for Publico in Lisbon. Banner, Headline, Text and Mono cuts across condensed widths.
description: An expansive serif system from Christian Schwartz and Paul Barnes with a long collaborator list, originating during work towards The Guardian's 2005 redesign (then named Hacienda) and finished for Mark Porter and Simon Esterson's redesign of Publico in Lisbon. Seven sub-families span Banner, Banner Condensed and Banner X Condensed for display, Headline and Headline Condensed for mastheads, Text for body, and Text Mono for tabular and code-adjacent work. Shares the skeleton of the Guardian typeface, making it a natural serif companion for newspapers, magazines and large editorial identities.
foundry_blurb: Publico originated as one of many stops on the long road to The Guardian's 2005 redesign and the Guardian family. Originally named Hacienda after the iconic Manchester nightclub, it was later finished for Mark Porter and Simon Esterson's redesign of Publico in Lisbon. Because it shares the skeletal form of the Guardian typeface, Publico is a perfect companion.

name: Review
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Review (originally Kippenberger) as a squarish, commanding display sans drawn by Berton Hasebe for the 2018 redesign of T: The New York Times Style Magazine. Hasebe sheared off overshoots and blunted exterior curves to produce strong horizontal bands of text, evoking embossing type without sentimentality.
summary: Berton Hasebe's squarish, commanding display sans drawn for T: The New York Times Style Magazine's 2018 redesign, with Condensed, Poster and Wide companions.
description: A four-width display sans drawn by Berton Hasebe with Tim Ripper, originally created as Kippenberger for the 2018 redesign of T: The New York Times Style Magazine when the publication shifted from image-led to text-led. Seven weights run from Thin to Black with italics across Review, Review Condensed, Review Poster and Review Wide. Overshoots are sheared off and exterior curves blunted, producing strong horizontal bands of letters that evoke embossing type while staying legible. Suits magazines, fashion, culture and identity systems that want a commanding voice.
foundry_blurb: Berton Hasebe originally drew Review (nee Kippenberger) for T: The New York Times Style Magazine. In 2018, a new editor in chief pushed for a complete reimagining of the magazine. What had primarily been an image-focused publication evolved into a text-driven one, with the squarish, commanding Review doing much of the heavy lifting. To facilitate tight setting both horizontally and vertically, Hasebe sheared off Review's overshoots and blunted its exterior curves, producing a dynamic tension with its round counters. Set tight, these walls of letters form a dramatic text image while remaining pleasantly legible and readable.

name: Robinson
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Robinson as a contemporary sans drawing on calligraphic sans serifs like Warren Chappell's Lydian and R. Hunter Middleton's Samson, with a crisp grotesque-leaning roman and more explicitly calligraphic italics. Evokes mid-twentieth-century American book-cover lettering.
summary: A crisp, kinetic calligraphic sans by Greg Gazdowicz drawing on Lydian and Samson, with grotesque-leaning romans and explicitly calligraphic italics.
description: A calligraphic sans by Greg Gazdowicz, taking cues from Warren Chappell's Lydian and R. Hunter Middleton's Samson to make a contemporary sans that uses calligraphic construction as a source of visual interest without being explicitly calligraphic. Ten styles run from Light to Black with italics. The romans are plainspoken with grotesque proportions, while the italics show a more explicit calligraphic connection. Evokes American book-cover lettering from the middle of the twentieth century while feeling cleanly contemporary. Suits book publishing, editorial and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Inspired by calligraphic sans serifs like Warren Chappell's Lydian and R. Hunter Middleton's Samson, Greg Gazdowicz aimed to make a contemporary sans that used the hallmarks of calligraphic construction to add visual interest without being explicitly calligraphic. The result is a crisp, refreshing sans with a kinetic personality. Robinson evokes American book-cover lettering from the middle of the twentieth century while feeling cleanly contemporary. The romans are plainspoken, with proportions more typical of a grotesque than written forms. The italics show a more explicit connection to calligraphy, but have the same dry, stripped-down approach to details seen in the romans.

name: Royal Gothic
designer: Paul Barnes with Luke Charsley and Tim Ripper
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Royal Gothic as a charming version of a boxy sans serif first seen around 1880 in Britain, expanded from a single original style into a seven-weight family with matching italics. Most lower case letters are square in form, the capitals are compact and the s remains grotesque.
summary: Paul Barnes's seven-weight revival of an 1880s British boxy sans, with compact capitals, square lower case and a still-grotesque s.
description: A fourteen-style revival sans by Paul Barnes with Luke Charsley and Tim Ripper, extending a single boxy sans first seen in Britain around 1880 into Thin, Light, Regular, Medium, Bold, Black and Fat with matching italics. Most lower-case letters are square, the capitals are compact, and the s stays decidedly grotesque. The original style appeared across Britain, Europe and the United States and stayed in circulation deep into the twentieth century. Suits Victorian and Americana-leaning identities, packaging and editorial display work.
foundry_blurb: Royal Gothic is a charming version of a boxy sans serif that first appeared around 1880 in Britain, now expanded from a single style into a seven-weight family with matching italics. Drawn by Paul Barnes with the assistance of Luke Charsley and Tim Ripper, this updated version both perfectly encapsulates its era and makes itself available for today's more complex typographic requirements. Royal Gothic's origins are somewhat obscure: though first found in London, it had a distinctly American name. It quickly appeared across both Europe and the United States and remained in circulation far into the twentieth century.

name: Sanomat
classification_notes: Commercial Type describe Sanomat as a hybrid serif with flared sans terminals, influenced by serifless romans like Optima and Albertus. Drawn for Sami Valtere's 2013 tabloid redesign of Helsingin Sanomat, with humanist curves echoing Finnish twentieth-century architecture.
summary: Paul Barnes's hybrid serifless roman drawn for the 2013 tabloid redesign of Helsingin Sanomat, with humanist curves echoing Finnish design and a wide multi-width family.
description: A four-width family by Paul Barnes, drawn as the primary headline face for Sami Valtere's 2013 redesign of Helsingin Sanomat when Finland's largest daily moved from broadsheet to tabloid. Seven weights run from Light to Black with italics across Sanomat, Sanomat Condensed, Sanomat X Condensed and Sanomat Text, plus variable fonts. Sits as a hybrid serif with flared sans terminals, in the lineage of Optima and Albertus, with humanist curves that echo Finnish twentieth-century architecture and design. Suits newspapers, fashion, beauty and any editorial system that wants warm authority.
foundry_blurb: Paul Barnes drew Sanomat as the primary headline typeface for Sami Valtere's 2013 redesign of the Helsingin Sanomat, the most widely read quality national daily newspaper in Finland. Sanomat is an unusual hybrid: a serif face with flared sans terminals, influenced by serifless romans like Optima and Albertus. This almost inscriptional quality helps the typeface look serious and decidedly upscale, important for maintaining the feel of a quality broadsheet in the tabloid format. The family's gentle curves and humanist sentiment echo Finnish twentieth-century architecture and design. Although originally drawn for editorial work, Sanomat's distinctive personality adapts easily to other environments.

name: Sanomat Sans
classification_notes: Commercial Type's sans companion to Sanomat, drawn by Vincent Chan with Christian Schwartz and Miguel Reyes. The collection runs from a Display set with extreme weights, including a Stencil Black, through to a Text set tuned for running copy.
summary: The sans companion to Sanomat from Commercial Type, with a Display set running to a Stencil Black and a Text cut tuned for running copy.

Sanomat Sans Text trims the range to seven workhorse weights with italics, drawn for running copy at smaller sizes. foundry_blurb: Commercial Type credit Sanomat Sans to Vincent Chan with Christian Schwartz and Miguel Reyes. The collection is split into a Sanomat Sans Family for display with extreme weights including a Stencil Black, and a Sanomat Sans Text Family tuned for running copy.

name: Schnyder

name: Stag

classification_notes: Commercial Type list Styrene as designed by Berton Hasebe with Ilya Ruderman and Panagiotis Haratzopoulos. A geometric sans drawn against the Avant Garde lineage, with Styrene A as the rounder, double-storey companion and Styrene B as the squarer, single-storey display cut.
